## [00:00 - 03:03]

Hey everyone, it's Sam Ovens here and welcome to this module called Setting Client Expectations. So in this module, I'm going to show you how to set up and structure your client relationships the right way from day one. So when it comes to consulting and providing services, one thing I see consultants mess up all the time is the expectations. They either set them way too high, they don't set them at all, they don't have any sort of agreement in place or they overpromise too much. I mean there's all sorts of different mistakes that I see consultants make when dealing with their clients. And these mistakes really cost consultants a lot of time, a lot of frustration and a lot of money. If you don't structure your relationships with your clients correctly from day one, then things are pretty much guaranteed to fall over and cause trouble. So this training module is going to show you exactly how to set everything up, use agreements, structure everything correctly and really make sure that the way you work with clients is professional. So let's jump in and get started. So here's what we're going to cover on today's training. So we're going to start off with why all client issues derive from poor expectations. So why all client issues really begin here. Then we're going to talk about turning pro as a consultant, moving into the professional level and telling people how you do and don't work. So instead of just being an order taker and being at the mercy of your clients and whatever they want you to do, we're going to talk about how to turn pro and tell people what you will and won't do and how you do and don't work. Then I'm going to share with you the Mona Lisa of agreements. And this is a template which I have created for you so that you can set up your very own consulting agreement. So this is a basic agreement that you'll use with all of your different clients and when you sign them up, you'll adapt this agreement and then you'll get your client to sign it and hand it back to you before you start working with them. And this is what all professional consultants do because they state up front how everything's going to work, how the communications are going to go, what their responsibility is and all of that. And so I'm going to give you the ultimate agreement. And then we're going to discuss dividing responsibility. So what's on you and what's on them? A lot of consultants believe that everything's on them and that's not true. And some consultants think that everything's on the client and that's not true either. But really is a team effort and we're going to really have a conversation about how do you draw the line between what's on you and what's on the client and how do you know who is responsible, how do you deal with that sort of situation. And then finally, learning to say no and hold your ground.

there will always be trouble. And it doesn't matter what nature of client, doesn't matter how nice they are, how anything they are, their nature doesn't matter. ## [09:10 - 12:13]

without too much legal speak. So they're not these huge, multiple hundred page things. They clearly outline the specific services you're going to provide to the client. ## [24:21 - 27:21]

So that's a big question a lot of consultants ask. Should we offer a guarantee? And my answer to that is no. Don't. Because you really can't. You're really lying if you say that you can. ## [39:29 - 42:31]

clients all the time. When you're getting started, it's important to listen to these requests and determine what isn't, isn't necessary in order to achieve the outcome that you're promising. And you need to integrate requests that are necessary and decline everything that isn't necessary. And that's why you need to have a good filter in your mind. That's why you want to try and use first principles instead of reasoning by analogy because you have to get a good filter. What do I need to add and what do I not need to add? What do I say yes to? What do I say no to? But you're honestly going to be saying no pretty much all the time. You're going to get really good at saying no. So once you get to a point where you're in flow and delivering your clients amazing results, it's important that you deflect all additional requests to change the way you work unless it makes absolute sense. And you need to safeguard your magic recipe and your time so that you can help your clients. And this requires saying no. So in the early stages, you're going to have to listen to feedback and you are going to have to integrate some things into your offer. In the beginning, you just can't be like no, no, no, no, no to everyone because quite a lot of the time, I mean, you don't even know what you're doing yourself because you're just getting started and you're learning. So here you can say yes to a lot more things, but you still want to be conscious that you should also say no. But then once you get into a point where you're delivering results for people and you're in flow, that's when you want to start to say no all the time because you think about it like this. If we've got a process that's working and it's getting everyone results, why would we change it? Why would a client want us to mess with variables in a process that are working when it has a higher chance of breaking it than it does improving it? So this is when you want to start getting good at saying no. And when existing clients request additional things from you, you need to be strong and politely inform them that you do not offer what they're asking for. And people never get angry when you say no, but they always try it on when it comes to requesting more from you. So this is an important lesson. Everyone's going to try it on. And that's why you need to be good at saying no, but don't think that they're going to get angry. They knew you were probably going to say no anyway. So it's fine. ## [42:31 - 45:35]

who provide stunning results in their respective areas and then refer your clients to them when they ask for things you don't provide. And this way you look more professional, you help your client, and you deflect the request and build a referral partner for the future. So for example, if someone comes to me and they say, well, this happened to me all the time when I was doing consulting back in New Zealand, if someone came to me and they're like, I need help with SEO, I would say, hey, understand that. I know a little bit about it, but I'm not very good at it. And so I don't think you want me doing it. However, I do know one guy who's really good, and I'm happy to make a referral to you. I'm happy to refer him to you if you want. And that'll always be like, oh, yeah, that'd be amazing. Can you do that? So I'd make the referral, and then they got the SEO services. They were satisfied and everything, and they were happy, and they liked me because I referred them to the right person. And then I built up a friend and a connection so that when he got clients that came to him that wanted like AdWords or Facebook ads or whatever, he would send them my way. And so you start to build up referral partners. And so things come back to you. If you start helping someone out by sending them some referrals, well, they're going to help you out by sending you some referrals. That's just how it works. So that's a really powerful way to say no and help your client and build a referral partner all at the same time. It's kind of like hitting three birds with one stone. So it will feel uncomfortable and wrong at first, but it is the right thing to do. And you can't be a yes man and expect to achieve anything great in your life. By a yes man, I mean someone that just says yes to everything all the time. Oh yeah, I can do that. Yep, yep, we'll do that, no problem. You can't get good at anything if you do that. And in the modern day, it's more about what you ignore rather than what you pay attention to. So a lot of people, they think, well, I just need to learn more. I need to follow more things. I need to read more. I need to be on more people's lists. I need to read more newspapers, more magazines, more apps on my phone. I need to consume more. But that is a recipe for failure. In the modern day, it's more about what you ignore. You should be trying to add more things onto your ignore list. And you should have hardly anything on your pay attention to list. ## [45:35 - 47:13]

You know, people are going to ask you to do all sorts of things. Some of the ideas are going to be exciting. People are going to offer to pay you money to do things. And it probably sounds insane to a lot of people listening to this. You mean we say no to money? Yes. When you start making some money, if you're in survival mode, no. Then you don't say no to money. You take the money. And you do whatever the work is because you're in survival mode. However, once you do start making some money and you're out of survival mode, say no all the time. Turn down money. Now, I turn down so much money, it's ridiculous. But that's the only way I get to focus on something. And the focus on one thing and on doing what you're supposed to do, it will always end up serving you the best. It helps your clients and it helps their businesses. So they're going to pay you more money. You're going to get referrals because your clients are happy. So in the long run, you're going to make more money when you say no. In the short run, you make more money when you say yes. But when you say yes in the long run, you lose. So you get what I'm saying, right? If you say yes, you make more short term, but you lose long term. When you say no, you make less short term, but you make more long term. And we always want to play the long game, provided we're not in survival mode. So that's why this is a powerful quote that you want to remember.
